title: Sliding windows

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Sliding windows; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Sliding windows
sources.claim: A sliding window maintains an aggregate for a contiguous range while expanding and shrinking boundaries under a valid condition.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A sliding window maintains an aggregate for a contiguous range while expanding and shrinking boundaries under a valid condition.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A longest-distinct substring window removes left characters until the new right character has count one. Your goal is to implement and analyze fixed and variable sliding-window problems. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Arrays, strings and matrices; Hash tables, sets and maps; Stacks, queues and deques; Two-pointer and partition scans; Frequency and lookup patterns. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: A sliding window maintains an aggregate for a contiguous range while expanding and shrinking boundaries under a valid condition. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: Across C, C++, Java, and Python, keep the state transition and invariant language-neutral first; then map indexes, numeric overflow, recursion, mutable collections, hashing, and library operations to the selected language and retest their guarantees.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Negative values can break monotonic sum logic, fixed and variable windows differ, and counts must be updated symmetrically. The draft therefore rejects: Sliding-window sum logic works unchanged when arbitrary negative values are present.

includes: Maintain an incremental fixed or variable range summary instead of recomputing each interval.
Implement and analyze fixed and variable sliding-window problems.

Across C, C++, Java, and Python, keep the state transition and invariant language-neutral first; then map indexes, numeric overflow, recursion, mutable collections, hashing, and library operations to the selected language and retest their guarantees.
excludes: Negative values can break monotonic sum logic, fixed and variable windows differ, and counts must be updated symmetrically.

outcomes: Implement and analyze fixed and variable sliding-window problems.

examples.title: Sliding windows: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: A longest-distinct substring window removes left characters until the new right character has count one.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: A longest-distinct substring window removes left characters until the new right character has count one.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: The shrink rule needs a monotonic condition; negative contributions can invalidate common variable-window reasoning.
examples.result: The conceptual result follows because The shrink rule needs a monotonic condition; negative contributions can invalidate common variable-window reasoning.

examples.title: Sliding windows: implementation transfer
examples.situation: Across C, C++, Java, and Python, keep the state transition and invariant language-neutral first; then map indexes, numeric overflow, recursion, mutable collections, hashing, and library operations to the selected language and retest their guarantees.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Keep the same specification and invariant across all four languages.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: Across C, C++, Java, and Python, keep the state transition and invariant language-neutral first; then map indexes, numeric overflow, recursion, mutable collections, hashing, and library operations to the selected language and retest their guarantees.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: Negative values can break monotonic sum logic, fixed and variable windows differ, and counts must be updated symmetrically.
examples.result: Concept mastery transfers, while implementation evidence remains language-specific.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Sliding-window sum logic works unchanged when arbitrary negative values are present.
misconceptions.correction: The shrink rule needs a monotonic condition; negative contributions can invalidate common variable-window reasoning.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use an invariant or counterexample to explain why this DSA belief fails: Sliding-window sum logic works unchanged when arbitrary negative values are present.

analogy.example: A carefully labeled physical arrangement can introduce Sliding windows, while the canonical invariant and operations remain the actual algorithmic model.
usefulFor: Introducing structure, order, connectivity, or state transition before formal notation
limitations: A physical arrangement does not reproduce asymptotic cost, aliasing, numeric limits, recursion depth, or the four languages' implementation rules.
